content:
Consider the operator precedence and associativity rules for the integer arithmetic operators given in the table below.

OperatorPrecedenceAssociativity
+HighestLeft
−HighRight
*MediumRight
/LowRight

The value of the expression $3 + 1 + 5 * 2 / 7 + 2 − 4 − 7 − 6 / 2$ as per the above rules is _______
explanation:
First, solve the addition (+) operations using left associativity :

$$ \begin{aligned} & ((3 + 1) + 5) \times 2 / 7 + 2 - 4 - 7 - 6 / 2 \\\\ & 9 \times 2 / 7 + 2 - 4 - 7 - 6 / 2 \end{aligned} $$

Next, solve the subtraction (-) operations using right associativity :

$$ \begin{aligned} & 9 \times 2 / (7 + 2 - (4 - (7 - 6))) / 2 \\\\ & (9 \times 2) / 6 / 2 \\\\ & (18 / (6 / 2)) \quad \text{(Right associativity)} \\\\ & (18 / 3) = 6 \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct answer is 6.

content:
Consider the following sequence of operations on an empty stack.

push(54); push(52); pop(); push(55); push(62); s = pop();

Consider the following sequence of operations on an empty queue.

enqueue(21); enqueue(24); dequeue(); enqueue(28); enqueue(32); q = dequeue();

The value of s + q is ______
explanation:
To find the value of $ s + q $, we need to perform the given stack and queue operations step by step and record the values of $ s $ and $ q $.

Stack Operations:

push(54):

Stack: [54]

push(52):

Stack: [54, 52] (Top is 52)

pop():

Pops 52.

Stack: [54]

push(55):

Stack: [54, 55] (Top is 55)

push(62):

Stack: [54, 55, 62] (Top is 62)

$ s = $ pop():

Pops 62 and assigns to $ s $.

Stack: [54, 55]

Value of $ s $: 62

Queue Operations:

enqueue(21):

Queue: [21]

enqueue(24):

Queue: [21, 24]

dequeue():

Dequeues 21.

Queue: [24]

enqueue(28):

Queue: [24, 28]

enqueue(32):

Queue: [24, 28, 32]

$ q = $ dequeue():

Dequeues 24 and assigns to $ q $.

Queue: [28, 32]

Value of $ q $: 24

Compute $ s + q $:

$ s = 62 $

$ q = 24 $

$ s + q = 62 + 24 = 86 $

Answer: 86

content: A queue is implemented using an array such that ENQUEUE and DEQUEUE operations are performed efficiently. Which one of the following statements is CORRECT ($$n$$ refers to the number of items in the queue)?

options.content: Both operations can be performed in $$O\left( 1 \right)$$ time
At most one operation can be performed in $$O\left( 1 \right)$$ time but the worst case time for the other operation will be $$\Omega \left( n \right)$$
The worst case time complexity for both operations will be $$\Omega \left( n \right)$$
Worst case time complexity for both operations will be $$\Omega \left( {\log n} \right)$$

If a stack S contains the integers 2, -3, 2, -1, 2 in order from bottom to top, what is f(S)?
The best data structure to check whether an arithmetic expression has balanced parentheses is a

options.content: An operand stack and an operator stack

content:
Consider the following statements:
(i) First-in-first out types of computations are efficiently supported by STACKS.
(ii) Implementing LISTS on linked lists is more efficient than implementing LISTS on an array for almost all the basic LIST operations.
(iii) Implementing QUEUES on a circular array is more efficient than implementing QUEUES on a linear array with two indices.
(iv) Last-in-first-out type of computations are efficiently supported by QUEUES.

Consider a stack $S$ and a queue $Q$. Both of them are initially empty and have the capacity to store ten elements each. The elements $1,2,3,4$, and 5 arrive one by one, in that order. When an element arrives, it is assigned either to $S$ (pushed on $S$ ) or to $Q$ (enqueued to $Q)$. Once all the five elements are stored, the output is generated in two steps. First, stack $S$ is emptied by popping all elements. Then queue $Q$ is emptied by dequeueing all elements. The output obtained by following this process is 43125 .

Given the output, the objective is to predict whether an element was assigned to $S$ or $Q$. Which of the following options is/are possible valid assignment(s) of the elements?

Note: In the options, the notation $x S$ denotes that element $x$ was assigned to $S$ and $y Q$ denotes that element $y$ was assigned to $Q$.
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Option (a) }\\ &1 S, 2 Q, 3 S, 4 S, 5 Q \end{aligned} $$

POP the all elements from stack $S=4,3,1$

Dequeue the all element from queue $Q=2,5$

So, option (a) is correct.

Option (b)

$1 Q, 2 Q, 3 S, 4 S, 5 Q$

POP the all elements from stack $S=4,3$

Dequeue the all element from queue $Q=1,2,5$

Output $=4,3,1,2,5$

So, option (b) is valid.

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Option (c) }\\ &1 Q, 2 Q, 3 Q, 4 S, 5 S \end{aligned} $$

POP the all elements from stack $S=5,4$

Dequeue the all element from queue $Q=1,2,3$

Output $=5,4,1,2,3$

Output sequence does not matched.

So, option (c) is invalid.

Option (d)

$$ 1 S, 2 S, 3 S, 4 Q, 5 Q $$

POP the all elements from stack $S=3,2,1$

Dequeue the all element from queue $Q=4,5$

Output order $=3,2,1,4,5$,

Output order does not matched.

Option (d) is invalid.

content:
Consider a stack data structure into which we can PUSH and POP records. Assume that each record pushed in the stack has a positive integer key and that all keys are distinct. We wish to augment the stack data structure with an $\mathrm{O}(1)$ time MIN operation that returns a pointer to the record with smallest key present in the stack

1. without deleting the corresponding record, and

2. without increasing the complexities of the standard stack operations.

Which one or more of the following approach(es) can achieve it?

options.content: Keep with every record in the stack, a pointer to the record with the smallest key below it.
Keep a pointer to the record with the smallest key in the stack.
Keep an auxiliary array in which the key values of the records in the stack are maintained in sorted order.
Keep a Min-Heap in which the key values of the records in the stack are maintained.

explanation:
Let's analyze each option to see if it meets the requirements:

We need the MIN operation to run in $$\mathrm{O}(1)$$ time.

The standard stack operations (PUSH and POP) must remain with their usual time complexities (constant time).

The MIN operation should return a pointer to the record with the smallest key without deleting it.

Below is a breakdown of each approach:

• Option A – “Keep with every record in the stack, a pointer to the record with the smallest key below it.”

  - How it works: When pushing a new record, you compare its key with the current minimum (available from the top record’s pointer). You then store a pointer to the new minimum in the new record.

  - PUSH can be done in $$\mathrm{O}(1)$$ time because it involves just one comparison and pointer update.

  - POP is still $$\mathrm{O}(1)$$ because you just remove the top record.

  - The MIN operation becomes trivial, as you just return the pointer stored with the top record.

  - This approach meets all requirements.

• Option B – “Keep a pointer to the record with the smallest key in the stack.”

  - How it works: You maintain a single pointer for the minimum.

  - The problem: When the record pointed to as minimum is popped, you need to find the next smallest record. Finding the new minimum could involve scanning the entire stack, which is not $$\mathrm{O}(1)$$.

  - Therefore, this approach fails to guarantee constant time operations.

• Option C – “Keep an auxiliary array in which the key values of the records in the stack are maintained in sorted order.”

  - How it works: You keep an array sorted by keys.

  - The problem: Inserting into or removing from a sorted array involves shifting elements, which takes $$\mathrm{O}(n)$$ time in the worst case.

  - Thus, it increases the complexity of standard stack operations, making it unsuitable.

• Option D – “Keep a Min-Heap in which the key values of the records in the stack are maintained.”

  - How it works: A Min-Heap allows retrieval of the smallest element in $$\mathrm{O}(1)$$ time.

  - The problem: Inserting into and deleting from a heap are $$\mathrm{O}(\log n)$$ operations, which increases the complexities of standard stack operations.

  - So, this approach does not meet the required complexity constraints.

Conclusion: Only Option A meets all the conditions, providing constant time operations for PUSH, POP, and MIN.

Thus, the correct answer is Option A.

content:
Let S1 and S2 be two stacks. S1 has capacity of 4 elements. S2 has capacity of 2 elements. S1 already has 4 elements: 100, 200, 300, and 400, whereas S2 is empty, as shown below.

Stack S1
400 (Top)
300
200
100
Stack S2

Only the following three operations are available:

PushToS2: Pop the top element from S1 and push it on S2.
PushToS1: Pop the top element from S2 and push it on S1.
GenerateOutput: Pop the top element from S1 and output it to the user.

Note that the pop operation is not allowed on an empty stack and the push operation is not allowed on a full stack.

Which of the following output sequences can be generated by using the above operations?
explanation:
Option B :

Sequence: 200, 300, 400, 100

Pop 400 and 300 from S1 and push them to S2.

   S1 = [200, 100], S2 = [300, 400]

    Output 200 from S1.

    Pop 300 from S2 and push it to S1.

    Output 300 from S1.

       S1 = [100], S2 = [400]

      Pop 400 from S2 and push it to S1.

      Output 400, then 100 from S1.

         Output: 200, 300, 400, 100

      Option C:


      Sequence: 400, 200, 100, 300


      Output 400 from S1.

      Pop 300 from S1 and push it to S2.

         S1 = [200, 100], S2 = [300]

        Output 200 and then 100 from S1.

        Pop 300 from S2 and push it to S1.

        Output 300 from S1.

           Output: 400, 200, 100, 300

        Option D:


        Sequence: 300, 200, 400, 100


        Pop 400 from S1 and push it to S2.

           S1 = [300, 200, 100], S2 = [400]

          Output 300 and 200 from S1.

          Pop 400 from S2 and push it to S1.

          Output 400 and then 100 from S1.

             Output: 300, 200, 400, 100

          Hence, the correct options are B, C, and D.

content:
Consider a sequence a of elements $$a_0=1,a_1=5,a_2=7,a_3=8,a_4=9$$, and $$a_5=2$$. The following operations are performed on a stack S and a queue Q, both of which are initially empty.

I: push the elements of a from a$$_0$$ to a$$_5$$ in that order into S.

II: enqueue the elements of a from a$$_0$$ to a$$_5$$ in that order into Q.

III: pop an element from S.

IV: dequeue an element from Q.

V: pop an element from S.

VI: dequeue an element from Q.

VII: dequeue an element from Q and push the same

VIII: Repeat operation VII three times.

IX: pop an element from S.

X: pop an element from S.

The top element of S after executing the above operations is ____________.
explanation:
To determine the top element of the stack $S$ after executing the given series of operations, we will follow each operation step-by-step.

Initial Sequences:

Sequence $a$: $a_0 = 1$, $a_1 = 5$, $a_2 = 7$, $a_3 = 8$, $a_4 = 9$, $a_5 = 2$

Operations:

I. Push the elements of $a$ from $a_0$ to $a_5$ in that order into $S$.

Stack $S$ (from bottom to top): 1, 5, 7, 8, 9, 2

II. Enqueue the elements of $a$ from $a_0$ to $a_5$ in that order into $Q$.

Queue $Q$ (from front to back): 1, 5, 7, 8, 9, 2

III. Pop an element from $S$.

Element popped: 2

Stack $S$ (from bottom to top): 1, 5, 7, 8, 9

IV. Dequeue an element from $Q$.

Element dequeued: 1

Queue $Q$ (from front to back): 5, 7, 8, 9, 2

V. Pop an element from $S$.

Element popped: 9

Stack $S$ (from bottom to top): 1, 5, 7, 8

VI. Dequeue an element from $Q$.

Element dequeued: 5

Queue $Q$ (from front to back): 7, 8, 9, 2

VII. Dequeue an element from $Q$ and push the same element into $S$.

Element dequeued and pushed: 7

Queue $Q$ (from front to back): 8, 9, 2

Stack $S$ (from bottom to top): 1, 5, 7, 8, 7

VIII. Repeat operation VII three times.

First repetition:

Element dequeued and pushed: 8

Queue $Q$ (from front to back): 9, 2

Stack $S$ (from bottom to top): 1, 5, 7, 8, 7, 8

Second repetition:

Element dequeued and pushed: 9

Queue $Q$ (from front to back): 2

Stack $S$ (from bottom to top): 1, 5, 7, 8, 7, 8, 9

Third repetition:

Element dequeued and pushed: 2

Queue $Q$: (from front to back): [empty]

Stack $S$ (from bottom to top): 1, 5, 7, 8, 7, 8, 9, 2

IX. Pop an element from $S$.

Element popped: 2

Stack $S$ (from bottom to top): 1, 5, 7, 8, 7, 8, 9

X. Pop an element from $S$.

Element popped: 9

Stack $S$ (from bottom to top): 1, 5, 7, 8, 7, 8

Thus, the top element of $S$ after executing all the operations is 8.

content: Consider the queues Q1 containing four elements and Q2 containing none (shown as the Initial State in the figure). The only operations allowed on these two queues are Enqueue (Q, element) and Dequeue (Q). The minimum number of Enqueue operations on Q1 required to place the elements of Q1 in Q2 in reverse order (shown as the Final State in the figure) without using any additional storage is ______________.
explanation:
Given : Queues $Q_1$ containing four elements and $Q_2$ containing none as shown below in the figure.

We have to obtain the following situation as shown below in the figure.

As we know that;

If queue is just having 2 elements then we can reverse a queue in place by just using simple enqueue and dequeue and if more than 2 elements we cannot reverse so, first divide 4 elements into 2-2 element each.

Step 1 : Enqueue 1 in $Q_2$, it is already sorted. Also Enqueue 2 in $Q_2$ i.e $1,2$.

Till now, No enqueue to $Q_1$.

Now reverse $Q_2$. This doesn't cost any Enqueue to $Q_1$.

Step 2 : Perform 1 Dequeue and 1 Enqueue simultaneously for 1 time 2 in $Q_2$ i.e. 2,1 (head pointing at 2 now)

Step 3 : Enqueue 3 in $Q_2$ i.e 2, 1, 3

Step 4 : Perform 1 Dequeue and 1 Enqueue simultaneously for 2 times in $Q_2$ i.e $2,1,3 \rightarrow$ $1,3,2 \rightarrow 3,2,1$ (head pointing at 3 now)

Step 5 : Enqueue 4 in $Q_2$ i.e $3,2,1,4$

Step 6 : Perform 1 Dequeue and 1 Enqueue simultaneously for 3 times in $Q_2$ i.e $3,2,1,4$

$$ \rightarrow 2,1,4,3 \rightarrow 1,4,3,2 \rightarrow 4,3,2,1 $$

So the number of enqueue operations in $Q_1$ is 0 .

Thus, we have obtain the final state as shown below

Hence, the correct answer is 0.

content: Suppose a stack implementation supports an instruction REVERSE, which reverses the order of elements on the stack, in addition to the PUSH and POP instructions. Which one of the following statements is TRUE with respect to this modified stack?

options.content: A queue cannot be implemented using this stack.
A queue can be implemented where ENQUEUE takes a single instruction and DEQUEUE takes a sequence of two instructions.
A queue can be implemented where ENQUEUE takes a sequence of three instructions and DEQUEUE takes a single instruction.
A queue can be implemented where both ENQUEUE and DEQUEUE take a single instruction each

content: Suppose a circular queue of capacity (n – 1) elements is implemented with an array of n elements. Assume that the insertion and deletion operations are carried out using REAR and FRONT as array index variables, respectively. Initially, REAR = FRONT = 0. The conditions to detect queue full and queue empty are

options.content:
full: (REAR+1)
mod n == FRONT
empty: REAR == FRONT

full: REAR == FRONT
empty: (REAR + 1)
mod n == FRONT

full: (FRONT+1)
mod n == REAR
empty: REAR == FRONT

content:
The following postfix expression with single digit operands is evaluated using a stack: 
8 2 3 ^ / 2 3 * + 5 1 * - 
Note that ^ is the exponentiation operator. The top two elements of the stack after the first * is evaluated are:

options.content: Reverses the order of the elements in the queue Q
Deletes the element at the front of the queue Q and inserts it at the rear keeping the other elements in the same order
$$n + m \le x \le 2n$$ and $$2m \le y \le n + m$$

explanation:
Let's analyze the number of push (x) and pop (y) operations based on the provided implementation of the queue using two stacks, S1 and S2. Let n be the total number of insert operations and m be the total number of delete operations, with m <= n.

Analysis of x (total push operations):

  1. insert(Q, X) function:

    • push(S1, X);

    • Each of the n insert operations performs exactly one push(S1, X). This contributes n to x.

  2. delete(Q) function (specifically the part involving push):

    • When S2 is empty and S1 is not empty, elements are transferred from S1 to S2:

      while (!(stack - empty(S1))){

  X = pop(S1);

  push(S2, X); // This is a push operation to S2

}

    • Maximum x: Each of the n distinct elements inserted into the queue is pushed onto S1 once. Subsequently, that same element might be moved from S1 to S2 (in which case it's pushed onto S2 once). An element can be pushed to S2 at most once in its lifetime, as after it's in S2, it's either deleted or remains there.

      • Thus, the total number of push(S1) operations is n.

      • The total number of push(S2) operations can be at most n (if all n elements are eventually transferred from S1 to S2).

      • Therefore, the maximum number of total push operations x is n + n = 2n.

      • x <= 2n.

    • Minimum x: There are always n push(S1) operations (one for each insert). Additionally, for each of the m successful delete operations, an element must eventually be popped from S2. To be eligible for pop(S2), that element must have been pushed onto S2 at some point. Therefore, at least m push(S2) operations are performed for the m successfully deleted elements.

      • Thus, x >= n (for S1) + m (for S2).

      • x >= n + m.

    • Combining these, we get: n + m <= x <= 2n.

Analysis of y (total pop operations):

  1. delete(Q) function (specifically the part involving pop):

    • The delete operation itself performs X = pop(S2); regardless of whether a transfer occurred (unless the queue is entirely empty). There are m successful delete operations. This contributes m to y.

    • When S2 is empty and S1 is not empty, elements are transferred from S1 to S2:

      while (!(stack - empty(S1))){

  X = pop(S1); // This is a pop operation from S1

  push(S2, X);

}

    • Maximum y: Each of the n distinct elements inserted into the queue can be popped from S1 at most once (because it moves to S2 and doesn't return).

      • Thus, the total number of pop(S1) operations can be at most n.

      • The total number of pop(S2) operations is exactly m (for m successful delete operations).

      • Therefore, the maximum number of total pop operations y is n + m.

      • y <= n + m.

    • Minimum y: Each of the m successfully deleted elements must have been popped from S2. Also, to be available in S2, these elements must have been transferred from S1 (as they are initially pushed to S1). This transfer implies they were popped from S1. Therefore, each of the m deleted elements contributes one pop(S1) and one pop(S2).

      • Thus, y >= m (from S1) + m (from S2).

      • y >= 2m.

    • Combining these, we get: 2m <= y <= n + m.

Conclusion:

Based on the analysis:

  • For pushes (x): n + m <= x <= 2n

  • For pops (y): 2m <= y <= n + m

This matches Option A.

The final answer is $\boxed{A}$

content: Assume that the operators +, -, ×, are left associative and ^ is right associative. The order of precedence (from highest to lowest) is ^, ×, +, -. The postfix expression corresponding to the infix expression a + b×c-d^e^f is

A program attempts to generate as many permutation as possible of the string “abcd” by pushing the character a,b,c,d in the same order onto a stack, but it may pop off the top character at any time. Which one of the following a strings CANNOT be generated using this program?

Let S be a stack of size n >= 1. Starting with the empty stack, suppose we push the first n natural numbers in sequence, and then perform n pop operations. Assume that Push and Pop operations take X seconds each, and Y seconds elapse between the end of one such stack operation and the start of the next operation. For m >= 1, define the stack-life of m as the time elapsed from the end of Push(m) to the start of the pop operation that removes m from S. The average stack-life of an element of this stack is

3 * log(x+1) - a/2

What value would the following function return for the input x = 95? 
 function fun (x:integer):integer; 
   Begin 
      If x > 100 then fun : x – 10 
      Else fun : fun(fun (x + 11)) 
   End;

A priority queue Q is used to implement a stack that stores characters. PUSH (C) is implemented INSERT (Q, C, K) where K is an appropriate integer key chosen by the implementation. POP is implemented as DELETEMIN(Q). For a sequence of operations, the keys chosen are in

The postfix expression for the infix expression 
A + B * (C + D) / F + D * E is:
Which of the following permutations can be obtained in the output (in the same order) using a stack assuming that the input is the sequence 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 in that order?

The following sequence of operations is performed on stack: 
PUSH (10),PUSH (20),POP,PUSH (10),PUSH (20),POP,POP,POP,PUSH (20),POP 
The sequence of the value popped out is:
